title: Unifying Plasticity in Ordered and Disordered Matter using Topological and Geometrical Descriptors
authors: Xin Wang, Yang Xu, Jin Shang, Yi Xing, Jie Zhang, Yujie Wang, Walter Kob, Matteo Baggioli
abstract: Identifying the regions responsible for plastic flow in amorphous solids remains an open problem, since structural disorder seems to prevent the direct application of concepts such as dislocations, topological defects that successfully describe irreversible deformations in crystalline systems. Here, we introduce fields of dislocation, disclination, and incompatibility densities, that reduce to the standard sources of plasticity in crystals and assess their predictive power in amorphous materials. We find that, in a simulated two-dimensional glass as well in two- and three-dimensional experimental granular systems, these fields exhibit strong spatial correlations with $D^2_{\text{min}}$, the standard measure used to locate plastic events under shear in disordered solids. Unlike $D^2_{\text{min}}$, these fields also allow to disentangle rotational and translational contributions to the plastic events, revealing that rotational defects becoming dominant in three dimensions. Our approach paves the way for a unified description of plasticity in crystalline and amorphous solids.
summary: This paper proposes a unified framework for understanding plasticity by applying continuous topological and geometric descriptors to both crystalline and amorphous materials. By treating defects as continuous fields rather than discrete singularities, the authors successfully bridge the gap between ordered and disordered systems. The findings show that these fields accurately predict plastic events and highlight the increasing importance of rotational defects in 3D systems.
main_problem: The difficulty of identifying the specific regions responsible for plastic flow in amorphous solids due to the lack of long-range order and the absence of discrete topological defects like dislocations found in crystals.
main_result: The introduction of continuous dislocation, disclination, and incompatibility density fields provides a unified description of plasticity that correlates strongly with standard measures like D2min and reveals that rotational defects become dominant in 3D.
method: The authors apply differential geometry to map particle displacement fields onto a grid, calculating continuous defect densities using the Nye tensor, Frank tensor, and Saint-Venant incompatibility tensor.
model_or_system: The study utilizes 2D simulated Lennard-Jones glasses, 2D experimental photoelastic granular disks, and 3D experimental 3D-printed plastic sphere packings under shear.
key_observables: Dislocation density, disclination density, incompatibility density, D2min (non-affine squared displacement), and Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM).
important_parameters: Shear strain (pre-yield vs. post-yield regimes), particle diameter (d), and noise amplitude (R).
main_assumptions: The approach treats discrete defects as continuous fields and assumes that the displacement field can be meaningfully interpolated onto a regular lattice.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows displacement fields overlaid with dislocation density; Figure 2 compares defect densities to D2min in 2D; Figure 3 quantifies SSIM correlation as a function of strain in 2D and 3D; Figure 4 shows stress-strain response across different platforms.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of plasticity in disordered matter, introduces the mathematical framework for continuous defect densities, presents results from 2D simulations and experiments, extends the findings to 3D systems, and concludes with a robustness analysis against noise and discretization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Defects in the displacement field. Representa- tive snapshots of the particle displacement field, overlaid on a heat map of the dislocation density |⃗α|, normalized by its maximum value. Left panel: 2D simulated Lennard-Jones glass with strain step ∆γ = 0.5%. Right panel: Experimen- tal 2D granular system with strain step ∆γ = 4.42%. In both panels, d denotes the particle diameter.

FIG. 2. Comparison between defect densities and D2 min in 2D. Heat maps of the dislocation (3), disclination (5), and incompatibility (6) densities, together with D2 min. For the dislocation density, we plot |⃗α|. (a) 2D Lennard-Jones glass at strain γ = 3.38% with ∆γ = 0.02%. (b) 2D experimental granular system at γ = 4.64% with ∆γ = 0.113%. In both panels, the dashed contours indicate the top-percentile clusters of D2 min. In both systems, d refers to the particle diameter.

FIG. 3. Quantifying the similarity between the fields. Structural similarity index measure (SSIM) as a function of strain for three different systems: (a) 2D simulation, (b) 2D experiment, and (c) 3D experiment. The solid lines represent averages over three independent samples, while the symbols show representative data with corresponding error bars. Vertical dashed lines indicate the location of the yielding transition.

FIG. 4. Mechanical properties under shear deformation. Stress ratio and force as a function of strain γ. (a) 2D simulation, (b) 2D experiment, and (c) 3D experiment. The insets show a schematic cartoon of the applied shear deformation for each case. In panels (a) and (b), σ denotes the shear stress and P the pressure. Vertical lines indicate the yielding strain.

FIG. 5. Comparison between defect densities and D2 min in 3D. Normalized norm of the (a) dislocation, (b) disclination, (c) incompatibility densities and (d) D2 min in 3D experimental granular system with γ = 38.33%. The samples are sheared in the x-direction.
